metadata.summary: Case studies and benchmarks across SaaS, e-commerce, mobile, and B2B funnels. Calibrate performance against industry good/great thresholds and identify proven optimization patterns.

content.title: Funnel Optimization Examples and Benchmarks
content.summary: Concrete before/after case studies across SaaS, e-commerce, mobile app, B2B lead gen, and subscription funnels, paired with industry conversion benchmarks by step. Use to calibrate whether your funnel is performing at good/great level and to identify proven fixes for common drop-off patterns.

why.p: Optimization decisions made without benchmarks lead to optimizing steps that are already performing well. This reference anchors diagnosis — if your SaaS visitor-to-signup is 8%, it is already "great"; if it is 1%, the gap is 2-4x and warrants immediate attention.

list.item: Benchmarking current funnel metrics against industry standards
Identifying which common issues map to observed drop-off patterns
Looking for proven solution patterns before generating new hypotheses
Communicating optimization potential to stakeholders with real-world uplift data
Your product category is not represented (niche B2B, marketplace, etc.) — benchmarks may not apply
Using benchmarks as targets without accounting for your audience and traffic quality
Substituting case study results for your own A/B test data
Single-product, low-traffic situations where benchmarks are statistically meaningless

how-to.step: Identify your funnel type and locate it in the benchmarks section
Extract your current conversion rate at each step
Compare your rate against the "Good" and "Great" bands for your industry
Bucket your rate as below_good, good, or great
If below_good, find the matching case study and review the proven optimization sequence
Use the issue-patterns section to map your specific drop-off to root causes
Remember: case studies are illustrative, not prescriptive — root cause varies by context

section.name: case-studies
section.p: Five before/after case studies demonstrating funnel optimization outcomes across SaaS, e-commerce, mobile, B2B, and subscription products.

example.title: SaaS Signup Funnel
example.note: Problem: 2% overall conversion. Homepage to Pricing: 80% drop (value prop unclear). Pricing to Signup: 60% drop (confusing pricing). Fixes: clearer homepage headline (+15% to pricing), simplified pricing 3 to 2 tiers (+25% signup), "Start free" emphasis (+10% trial start). Result: 2.0% to 3.8% overall (+90%).

example.title: E-commerce Checkout
example.note: Problem: 60% cart abandonment. Root causes by share: 35% shipping cost surprise, 25% account creation required, 20% payment form friction. Fixes: show shipping on product page (-15% abandonment), add guest checkout (-20% abandonment), express checkout (Apple Pay/Google Pay) (+12% completion).

example.title: Mobile App Onboarding
example.note: Problem: Only 15% of installs reach core action. Onboarding had 3 screens causing progressive drop. Solution: reduce to 1 welcome screen, skip directly to core action, show tips contextually. Result: 15% to 42% core action rate (+180%).

example.title: B2B Lead Generation
example.note: Problem: 0.35% blog-to-demo conversion. Fixes: CTA changed from "Download whitepaper" to "Get free checklist" (+25% clicks), form fields reduced from 8 to 3 (+40% completion), A/B tested email subjects (+15% open rate), added demo video in email (+20% demo requests). Result: 0.35% to 0.98% (+180%).

example.title: Subscription Trial
example.note: Problem: 5% trial-to-paid conversion. Users not experiencing value in first 3 days; no reminder emails; payment friction. Fixes: onboarding email sequence Days 1/3/5/7 (+20% Day-7 active), time-to-value checklist in app (+15% activation), pre-authorize payment card (+25% conversion). Result: 5% to 9.5% (+90%).

section.name: benchmarks
section.p: Good vs. great conversion rate thresholds per step for SaaS, e-commerce, mobile apps, B2B lead gen, and email funnels.

list.item: Visitor to Signup: Good 2-5%, Great 5-10%
Signup to Activated: Good 30-50%, Great 50-70%
Activated to Paid: Good 3-5%, Great 5-10%
Visitor to Paid (overall): Good 0.5-1%, Great 1-3%
Visitor to Cart: Good 3-8%, Great 8-15%
Cart to Checkout: Good 40-60%, Great 60-80%
Checkout to Purchase: Good 50-70%, Great 70-85%
Visitor to Purchase (overall): Good 1-2%, Great 2-4%
Install to Open: Good 70-80%, Great 80-90%
Open to Onboarding Complete: Good 40-60%, Great 60-80%
Onboarding to Core Action: Good 25-40%, Great 40-60%
Install to Core Action (overall): Good 20-30%, Great 30-50%
Visit to Lead Magnet Download: Good 5-10%, Great 10-20%
Form View to Submit: Good 30-50%, Great 50-70%
Lead to Qualified Lead: Good 20-30%, Great 30-50%
Qualified to Demo: Good 30-40%, Great 40-60%
Demo to Customer: Good 20-30%, Great 30-50%
Delivered to Opened: Good 15-25%, Great 25-40%
Opened to Clicked: Good 2-5%, Great 5-10%
Clicked to Converted: Good 1-3%, Great 3-8%
Delivered to Converted (overall): Good 0.1-0.5%, Great 0.5-2%

section.name: issue-patterns
section.p: Diagnostic patterns mapping observed drop-off symptoms to root causes and proven solution approaches.

list.item: High homepage bounce: value prop unclear — A/B test headlines, add social proof above fold
Form abandonment: too many fields — reduce to 3-5 critical fields only
Low activation: no guidance after signup — add onboarding checklist and contextual tooltips
Cart abandonment: surprise shipping costs — show total cost upfront on product page
Email verification drop: delayed or spam-filtered emails — improve deliverability, add resend option
Trial-to-paid drop: value not demonstrated — improve activation, add usage-based upgrade prompts
Mobile permission denial: scary permission prompts — explain why each permission is needed before requesting
Homepage to Pricing drop (60-80%): value prop unclear — clarify headline to one outcome
Trial to Activation drop (40-60%): onboarding friction — reduce steps, add aha-moment shortcut
Activated to Paid drop (90-95%): pricing or value mismatch — add upgrade prompts at limit moments
Product to Cart drop (80-90%): browsing intent, not buying intent — add urgency, clearer benefits
Cart to Checkout drop (40-60%): shipping or account friction — show costs early, offer guest checkout
Checkout to Payment drop (20-30%): trust or security concerns — add trust badges, security copy
Install to First Open drop (10-30%): permission request on launch — delay until after value shown
Onboarding screen drop (20-30% per screen): too much friction — cut to one essential screen
First Session to Return drop (50-70%): no habit formed — implement Day 1 push notification with re-engagement hook

antipattern.description: Optimizing steps that already meet "great" benchmarks while ignoring steps below "good."
antipattern.reason: Effort spent on a step at 80% conversion (already great) produces less gain than fixing a step at 20% conversion (well below good). Always benchmark first.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: Benchmarks are aggregate; subverticals and ICPs deviate substantially. SaaS "Visitor → Signup 2–5%" can be 0.5% (enterprise self-serve) or 15% (consumer SaaS).
The five worked examples are illustrative — agents must NOT copy the optimization sequence verbatim; root cause varies by product and market.
Numeric percentages (e.g., "+25% signup rate") are point estimates from past tests; they have wide confidence intervals and may not apply to your cohort.
Benchmarks predate any platform-specific shifts (iOS privacy changes, AI-search traffic dilution); some categories (mobile install → open) shift more than 10% year-over-year.
Email benchmarks are global averages; deliverability and segmentation effects dominate the listed bands. Do not treat email benchmarks as universal.
Always report the bucket (below_good / good / great) AND the gap to great — single-number lookups invite over-reaction.
Pair benchmark with confidence band (sample size, lookback) of your own data; don't compare last-week noise to industry averages.
For ecommerce, prefer higher-resolution checkout-step bands from Baymard's research when available, not summary benchmarks.
For mobile install→open, segment by paid vs organic — the bands diverge significantly post-iOS privacy changes.
For B2B, replace generic "Demo → Customer 20–30%" with sales-cycle-aware bands; the README's number may be stale for long enterprise cycles.
Refresh benchmarks annually; flag any benchmark older than 18 months as potentially stale.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A benchmarks agent is a read-only retriever. Orchestrator passes (industry, stage, current rate, ICP optional). The subagent matches the industry table, returns Good/Great/current bucket, and surfaces the closest worked example with its proven optimization sequence as a starting hypothesis (NOT a prescription).
A gap-narrative-writer produces a single-paragraph executive summary contextualizing performance against benchmarks.
A pattern-matcher scores similarity between current funnel shape and the five examples; if similarity is below threshold, it refuses to recommend.

Recommended subagents: benchmark-lookup (input: industry + stage + current rate; output: { bucket, gap_to_great, table_row }), pattern-matcher (input: current funnel + observed drops; output: { matched_example, similarity_score, optimization_sequence }), gap-narrative-writer (produces executive paragraph contextualizing performance against benchmarks), hand-off to funnel-basics-framework (diagnosis) and funnel-tactics-* (action).
